sections.Symbol:
Suit element: Earth / World: Assiah (Material). Pentacle = the material world of Assiah, the manifest form in which the three higher worlds crystallise. The disc bears the hexagram of Sol and Luna.

The Page (Princess in GD / Knave in TdM) is the earthy part of the suit element — Earth of Earth of Earth. She is the most material expression of the suit: the crystallised form, the heir, the one who brings the energy down into the physical world. In GD the Page corresponds to the final Heh of the Tetragrammaton, the manifest daughter.
sections.Scene: A young man in a flower-bordered tunic stands in a meadow, holding a pentacle at eye level with both hands and gazing at it intently. Mountains and a forested landscape surround him. He appears absorbed in study or contemplation of the symbol.
sections.Waite:
[A. E. Waite, *The Pictorial Key to the Tarot*, 1911 — a later esoteric reading describing the Rider–Waite–Smith imagery.]

PENTACLES

Page

A youthful figure, looking intently at the pentacle which hovers over his raised hands. He moves
slowly, insensible of that which is about him. Divinatory Meanings : Application, study,
scholarship, reflection another reading says news, messages and the bringer thereof; also rule,
management. Reversed : Prodigality, dissipation, liberality, luxury; unfavourable news.

items.name: The 22 Keys — The Paths of the Tree

sections.About the Keys: In the Golden Dawn the 22 trumps are the 22 paths joining the ten Sephiroth on the Tree of Life, each carrying a Hebrew letter and an astrological force. This is the heart of the tradition: the tarot as a map of the descent and return of spirit. Note the Golden Dawn order — Strength at VIII (Leo), Justice at XI (Libra) — the swap from the Marseille that Waite and Smith kept.
sections.How to Use: Read the trumps as paths, not just pictures: each is a stage and a doorway on the Tree.
sections.Symbol: The 22 Major Arcana are the 22 Paths of the Tree of Life — the channels of divine energy connecting the ten sephiroth. Each path is assigned a Hebrew letter, an element or planetary or zodiacal force, a colour (by the four GD colour scales), and a Tarot Trump. In GD practice the Trumps were used as visual keys for pathworking — the initiate contemplated the image to project consciousness along the corresponding path.
sections.Scene: The twenty-two major arcana of the Golden Dawn tradition are shown here with the Rider-Waite-Smith images that Arthur Edward Waite and Pamela Colman Smith created to embody the Golden Dawn teachings. Each image is a visual translation of esoteric symbolism intended to communicate meaning without verbal instruction.

items.name: The 36 Decans — Lords of the Minor Arcana

sections.About the Decans: The small cards 2–10 map to the 36 decans — the ten-degree divisions of the zodiac — each ruled by a planet and bearing a Book T title (the 'Lord of ...'). The number gives the Sephirah, the suit gives the World. This is what lets a Golden Dawn reader date and place a card in the wheel of the year.
sections.How to Use: Read each pip as Planet-in-Sign within a Sephirah: the decan is the weather, the Sephirah the depth, the suit the world.
sections.Symbol: The 36 Minor Arcana pip cards (2-10 of each suit) correspond to the 36 Decans of the zodiac — each decan is a 10° division of the ecliptic, ruled by a planet. The card's meaning is thus an intersection of sephirah (row in the GD table) and decan-lord (column). The four aces are placed at the poles of the elements; the sixteen court cards are the sub-elemental expressions of each suit.
sections.Scene: The thirty-six decans divide the zodiac wheel into ten-degree segments, each ruled by a planet according to the Chaldean system. In the Golden Dawn Book T system, each numbered pip card from Two through Ten corresponds to one decan. The RWS images for these cards were designed with the decan's planetary and zodiacal character in mind.

sections.About this Suit: The Wands, suit of Fire, unfolding through the Sephiroth in Atziluth (the Archetypal World). From the Ace (Kether, the root) down to the Ten (Malkuth, the suit made fully manifest), with the four courts as Fire's inner elements.
sections.How to Use: Follow the suit from Ace to Ten to watch Fire descend from pure potential into the fully realised world.
sections.Symbol: The Suit of Wands corresponds to the element Fire and the Kabbalistic world of Atziluth (the Archetypal or Divine World). In the GD colour scale Wands are attributed to the King Scale — the purest, most saturated colours. The wand as magical instrument is the will in its most essential, undifferentiated form: the staff of the magician that directs without yet becoming anything specific.
sections.Scene: The Wands suit in the Golden Dawn Book T system corresponds to the element of Fire and to the Yod (first letter) of the Tetragrammaton. The RWS images show scenes of action, ambition, conflict, and creative force consistent with the fiery nature of the suit.

sections.About this Suit: The Cups, suit of Water, unfolding through the Sephiroth in Briah (the Creative World). From the Ace (Kether, the root) down to the Ten (Malkuth, the suit made fully manifest), with the four courts as Water's inner elements.
sections.How to Use: Follow the suit from Ace to Ten to watch Water descend from pure potential into the fully realised world.
sections.Symbol: The Suit of Cups corresponds to the element Water and the Kabbalistic world of Briah (the Creative World). In the GD colour scale Cups are attributed to the Queen Scale. The cup as vessel receives and holds — it is the chalice of the Grail, the alchemical flask in which transformation occurs. Water seeks its own level; feeling seeks its own depth.
sections.Scene: The Cups suit corresponds to the element of Water and to the He (second letter) of the Tetragrammaton. The RWS images portray emotional, relational, and imaginative scenes — unions, dreams, grief, and contentment — all expressing the watery, receptive quality of this suit.

sections.About this Suit: The Swords, suit of Air, unfolding through the Sephiroth in Yetzirah (the Formative World). From the Ace (Kether, the root) down to the Ten (Malkuth, the suit made fully manifest), with the four courts as Air's inner elements.
sections.How to Use: Follow the suit from Ace to Ten to watch Air descend from pure potential into the fully realised world.
sections.Symbol: The Suit of Swords corresponds to the element Air and the Kabbalistic world of Yetzirah (the Formative World). In the GD colour scale Swords are attributed to the Prince Scale. The sword divides, names, and judges — it is the instrument of the Demiurge who separates light from darkness, above from below. Air is the breath of the divine Name; the sword is its discriminating power.
sections.Scene: The Swords suit corresponds to the element of Air and to the Vav (third letter) of the Tetragrammaton. The RWS images show scenes of conflict, loss, mental clarity, and severance, all expressing the sharp, discriminating nature of air as force of mind.

sections.About this Suit: The Pentacles, suit of Earth, unfolding through the Sephiroth in Assiah (the Material World). From the Ace (Kether, the root) down to the Ten (Malkuth, the suit made fully manifest), with the four courts as Earth's inner elements.
sections.How to Use: Follow the suit from Ace to Ten to watch Earth descend from pure potential into the fully realised world.
sections.Symbol: The Suit of Pentacles (Disks/Coins) corresponds to the element Earth and the Kabbalistic world of Assiah (the Material World). In the GD colour scale Pentacles are attributed to the Princess Scale. The pentacle bears the hexagram at its centre — Sol and Luna combined in the material plane, the macrocosm sealed within the microcosm. Earth is the crystallisation of all three higher worlds into tangible form.
sections.Scene: The Pentacles suit corresponds to the element of Earth and to the final He of the Tetragrammaton. The RWS images portray material life — work, wealth, nature, and the body — grounded in earthly experience and the physical world.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “minor arcana” (40).
sections.Scene: The fifty-six minor arcana of the Book T system encode the full range of human experience across four elements and a hierarchy of social roles. The pip cards map the arc of each element from pure potential (Ace) through culmination (Ten); the court cards embody the social and elemental personalities that inhabit each suit.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “decan” (36).
sections.Scene: The thirty-six decan attributions connect each numbered pip card to a ten-degree segment of the zodiac and a planetary ruler. In the Book T system, the image and meaning of each pip card encode both the element of its suit and the specific quality of the decan it governs.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “major arcana” (22).
sections.Scene: The twenty-two major arcana in the Golden Dawn system correspond to the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet and the twenty-two paths of the Kabbalistic Tree of Life. The RWS images were designed to encode these correspondences in visual form accessible to initiates and students alike.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “court card” (16).
sections.Scene: The sixteen court cards in the Book T system represent elemental sub-types within each suit. The Page (Princess), Knight (Prince), Queen, and King of each suit blend the suit's primary element with a secondary elemental quality, producing a matrix of sixteen elemental personalities.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “air” (15).
sections.Scene: Air is the element of the Swords suit in the Book T system. It governs intellect, communication, conflict, and the discriminating power of mind. The Hebrew letter Vav (and the Ruach, or intellect, of the soul) are associated with this element in Golden Dawn teaching.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “water” (15).
sections.Scene: Water is the element of the Cups suit in the Book T system. It governs feeling, imagination, and the unconscious. In the Golden Dawn schema water corresponds to the He of the Tetragrammaton and to the emotional capacity of the soul.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “cups” (14).
sections.Scene: The Cups suit governs the realm of emotional life, dreams, and relationships. In Book T teaching, cups are associated with water and the He of YHVH. RWS images in this suit tend toward interior scenes of feeling and connection.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “earth” (14).
sections.Scene: Earth is the element of the Pentacles suit. It governs the physical world, material resources, practical skill, and the body. In Golden Dawn teaching earth corresponds to the final He of the Tetragrammaton and to the Assiah (physical) world.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “fire” (14).
sections.Scene: Fire is the element of the Wands suit. It governs will, creativity, ambition, and the life force. In Golden Dawn teaching fire corresponds to the Yod of the Tetragrammaton and to the divine creative impulse.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “pentacles” (14).
sections.Scene: The Pentacles suit governs the material world — resources, craft, skill, nature, and the body. In Book T the pentacle is the Earth symbol of the four tools of magic, corresponding to the physical plane (Assiah) and the final He of YHVH.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “swords” (14).
sections.Scene: The Swords suit governs the life of the mind and social conflict. In Book T, swords correspond to Air and Vav, the connecting principle of the Tetragrammaton. RWS Swords images are often scenes of difficulty, clarity, or decisive action.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “wands” (14).
sections.Scene: The Wands suit governs will, ambition, and creative action. In Book T, wands correspond to Fire and Yod, the initial divine impulse. RWS Wands images show figures in motion, striving, competing, or leading.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “knight” (8).
sections.Scene: In the Book T system the Knight corresponds to what later decks call the King — the most active and mobile expression of the court. Each Knight combines the element of his suit with the sub-element of Fire, producing a fiery, initiating version of that element.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “queen” (8).
sections.Scene: The Queen embodies the watery, receptive aspect of each suit element. In the Book T system the Queen is associated with the He of the Tetragrammaton at the elemental level — she receives and nurtures the creative impulse of the Knight.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “ace” (4).
sections.Scene: The Ace of each suit in the Book T system represents the Root of its element in its purest, most undivided form. The Ace is the first emanation of the Sephirah Kether into each of the four elemental worlds.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “king” (4).
sections.Scene: In the original Book T system what later decks label King is the Knight — the most active court figure. In adapted Golden Dawn decks the King sits at the pinnacle of the court hierarchy, expressing the solar, authority-bearing aspect of each element.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “page” (4).
sections.Scene: The Page (or Princess in Book T terminology) represents the earthly expression of each suit element — the grounded, practical, receptive level at which the element manifests in daily life.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “prince” (4).
sections.Scene: The Prince corresponds to what Waite calls the Knight — the airy, intellectual, mobile aspect of each suit element. In the Book T system the Prince (son of the Knight and Queen) traverses the element with speed and mental agility.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “princess” (4).
sections.Scene: The Princess is the earthly daughter-figure in the Book T court system. She represents the element in its most material and practical form, closest to the physical world and farthest from its original divine fire.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
sections.Scene: Browse the Golden Dawn Book T tarot by keyword — suit, element, number, court rank, decan attribution, and thematic grouping.
